review_text: There isn't much to say here. It's just super good.

This is a masterpiece based on a tragic chapter of history. It shows how a businessman's ambition evolved into a mission to save lives, fueled by profound compassion. While the soldiers seemed to have lost their humanity, intoxicated by the power to decide who lived or died, they also appeared driven by fear—the fear that showing kindness would be seen as a betrayal of their comrades. The final scene, where Schindler weeps because he feels he could have saved even more lives, is truly heart-wrenching. It reminds us that wealth is meaningless compared to the sanctity of life. I sincerely hope that these historical memories continue to be transformed into art, ensuring we remember and learn from the past.

I have watched this movie a few times over the years. To me, it is a necessary reminder of human history. It still boggles my mind that Spielberg was able to work on this movie about the Holocaust at the same time he was also working on a science fiction movie about dinosaurs. The emotional tones of the two movies could not be any farther apart.

Everyone should watch this touching, unforgettable masterpiece. It is one of those rare films that stays with you forever and forces you to reflect on humanity, cruelty, and compassion. Honestly, it should be mandatory viewing in junior high so younger generations understand this part of history. For me, it is the best movie ever made, or at the very least in the top three.
Gruesome events are rendered with masterful precision and elevated by performances of raw, unflinching intensity. A formidable cinematic achievement—yet one I probably shall not revisit.

"Schindler's List" is a classic film that depicts a significant historical event, all presented in black-and-white. With a runtime exceeding three hours, it offers a profound narrative experience. In my opinion, the film's direction is exceptional, and the casting choices are commendable, making it a notable cinematic achievement of its era. However, I find it challenging to revisit; the film's impact feels diminished in today's context. Given the advancements in filmmaking as of 2025, the stark black-and-white visuals and slower pacing seem out of place. Had I seen it when it first released, I might have appreciated it more. Watching it now, especially amidst a cinematic landscape rich with special effects and innovative storytelling, I felt it lacked the engaging elements I typically enjoy. This isn't to say it's a poor film; rather, it simply didn't capture my interest throughout its lengthy duration. Despite its acclaim and cultural significance, I found it wasn't quite my style.
One of the best movies ever made! Best movie about WW2

A film so powerful it moves into the dimension of transcendent art. So many iconic characters and scenes it’s hard to believe that at one point this was simply advertised as Spielberg’s latest film you could casually go see in the cinemas for the first time. A potent reminder of the true power of film to pierce into the core of the human experience and spirit.

It took me 30 years to bring myself to watch this film. It's brilliant, haunting and horrifying at the same time. I do have one tiny quibble though. The ending scene before he drives away elevates Schindler to a Christ-like character. That wasn't necessary to complete the narrative.
